stem: Let \(D\) be the region \( \{ (x,y) : 0 \le x \le [[b]],\ 0 \le y \le [[m]]x \} \). Evaluate \( \iint_D ([[p]]x + [[q]]y)\,dA = \int_{0}^{[[b]]} \int_{0}^{[[m]]x} ([[p]]x + [[q]]y)\,dy\,dx \).
solution: Fix \(x\) and integrate in \(y\) from \(0\) to \([[m]]x\): \( \int_{0}^{[[m]]x}([[p]]x+[[q]]y)\,dy = [[p]]x([[m]]x) + [[q]]\frac{([[m]]x)^2}{2} \). Then integrate this in \(x\) from \(0\) to \([[b]]\) to get \([[answer]]\).
distractor_rationales: This integrates \(y\) up to the constant \([[m]]\cdot[[b]]\) (a rectangle) instead of up to the line \(y=[[m]]x\).
This makes a sign error on the \(y\)-term after integrating.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Let \(D\) be the region \( \{ (x,y) : 0 \le x \le [[b]],\ 0 \le y \le [[k]]x^2 \} \), bounded above by a parabola. Evaluate \( \iint_D ([[p]]x + [[q]]y)\,dA = \int_{0}^{[[b]]} \int_{0}^{[[k]]x^2} ([[p]]x + [[q]]y)\,dy\,dx \).
solution: Fix \(x\) and integrate in \(y\) from \(0\) to \([[k]]x^2\): \( \int_{0}^{[[k]]x^2}([[p]]x+[[q]]y)\,dy = [[p]]x([[k]]x^2) + [[q]]\frac{([[k]]x^2)^2}{2} \). Then integrate this in \(x\) from \(0\) to \([[b]]\): \( \int_0^{[[b]]}\left([[p]]\cdot[[k]]x^3 + \tfrac{[[q]]\cdot[[k]]^2}{2}x^4\right)dx = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This treats the upper bound as if it were the linear curve \([[k]]x\) instead of the parabola \([[k]]x^2\), giving the wrong powers of \(x\).
This drops the factor of \(\tfrac12\) that comes from integrating the \(y\)-term.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Let \(D\) be the Type II region described by: for each \(y\) with \(0 \le y \le [[d]]\), \(x\) ranges from \(y^2\) to \([[d]]^2\) (so \(x\) is given as a function of \(y\), bounded by the parabola \(x=y^2\)). Evaluate \( \iint_D ([[p]]x + [[q]]y)\,dA = \int_{0}^{[[d]]} \int_{y^2}^{[[d]]^2} ([[p]]x + [[q]]y)\,dx\,dy \).
solution: Fix \(y\) and integrate in \(x\) first: \( \int_{y^2}^{[[d]]^2}([[p]]x+[[q]]y)\,dx = [[p]]\dfrac{[[d]]^4-y^4}{2} + [[q]]y([[d]]^2-y^2) \). Then integrate this in \(y\) from \(0\) to \([[d]]\) to get \([[answer]]\).
distractor_rationales: This mis-integrates the \(x\)-term as if the region were a rectangle \(0\le x\le [[d]]^2\), missing the \(y^4\) subtraction.
This drops the leading coefficients \(\tfrac12\) and \(\tfrac14\) that come from the antiderivatives.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Let \(D\) be the band-shaped region between two parallel lines, \( \{ (x,y) : 0 \le x \le [[b]],\ x \le y \le x+[[c]] \} \). Evaluate \( \iint_D ([[p]]x + [[q]]y)\,dA = \int_{0}^{[[b]]} \int_{x}^{x+[[c]]} ([[p]]x + [[q]]y)\,dy\,dx \).
solution: Fix \(x\) and integrate in \(y\) from \(x\) to \(x+[[c]]\): \( \int_{x}^{x+[[c]]}([[p]]x+[[q]]y)\,dy = [[p]]x[[c]] + [[q]]\dfrac{(x+[[c]])^2-x^2}{2} \). Simplify and integrate over \(x\) from \(0\) to \([[b]]\) to get \([[answer]]\).
distractor_rationales: This drops the \(q\)-term contribution entirely, keeping only the \(p\)-term.
This forgets the extra \(q[[c]]^2/2\) piece that comes from expanding \((x+[[c]])^2-x^2\).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Let \(D\) be the quarter-disk region \( \{ (x,y) : 0 \le x \le [[R]],\ 0 \le y \le \sqrt{[[R]]^2-x^2} \} \). Evaluate \( \iint_D ([[p]]x + [[q]]y)\,dA = \int_{0}^{[[R]]} \int_{0}^{\sqrt{[[R]]^2-x^2}} ([[p]]x + [[q]]y)\,dy\,dx \) directly in Cartesian coordinates.
solution: Integrating in \(y\) first, \( \int_0^{\sqrt{[[R]]^2-x^2}}([[p]]x+[[q]]y)\,dy = [[p]]x\sqrt{[[R]]^2-x^2} + [[q]]\dfrac{[[R]]^2-x^2}{2} \). Both pieces integrate cleanly (the first by the substitution \(u=[[R]]^2-x^2\), the second as a polynomial) over \(0\le x\le [[R]]\), giving \( \dfrac{([[p]]+[[q]])[[R]]^3}{3} = [[answer]] \); no \(\pi\) appears since neither piece is a circular-area term.
distractor_rationales: This mistakenly brings in a factor of \(\pi\), confusing this with a polar-coordinates area computation.
This drops the correct coefficient \(\tfrac13\), using \(\tfrac14\) instead.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Let \(D\) be the region enclosed between the parabola \(y=x^2\) and the line \(y=[[m]]x\) (the two curves meet at \(x=0\) and \(x=[[m]]\)): \( \{ (x,y) : 0 \le x \le [[m]],\ x^2 \le y \le [[m]]x \} \). Evaluate \( \iint_D ([[p]]x + [[q]]y)\,dA = \int_{0}^{[[m]]} \int_{x^2}^{[[m]]x} ([[p]]x + [[q]]y)\,dy\,dx \).
solution: Fix \(x\) and integrate in \(y\) from \(x^2\) to \([[m]]x\): \( \int_{x^2}^{[[m]]x}([[p]]x+[[q]]y)\,dy = [[p]]x([[m]]x-x^2) + [[q]]\dfrac{([[m]]x)^2-x^4}{2} \). Integrating this in \(x\) from \(0\) to \([[m]]\) gives \([[answer]]\).
distractor_rationales: This treats the lower bound as \(0\) instead of the parabola \(x^2\), forgetting to subtract it.
This uses the wrong exponents when expanding \(([[m]]x)^2-x^4\), as if the region were bounded by \(0\) below.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.
